Abaddon.pronunciation: ABAD'DON, [Heb. Ch. Syr. Sam. to be lost, or destroyed, to perish.]

definitions.text:
1. The destroyer, or angel of the bottomless pit. Revelation 9:11.
2. The bottomless pit.

Abase.pronunciation: ABA'SE, [Fr abaisser, from bas, low, or the bottom; W. bais; Latin and Gr. basis; Eng. base; It. Abbassare; Sp. bare, low. See Abash.]

definitions.text:
1. The literal sense of abase is to lower or depress, to throw or cast down, used by Bacon, 'to abase the eye.' But the word is seldom used in reference to material things.
2. To cast down; to reduce low; to depress; to humble; to degrade; applied to the passions, rank, office, and condition in life. Those that walk in pride he is able to abase Daniel 4:37. Whosoever exalteth himself shall be abased, Matthew 23:12. Job 40:11. 2 Corinthians 11:7.

Reduced to low state, humbled, degraded. In heraldry, it is used of the wings of eagles, when the tops are turned downwards towards the point of the shield; or when the wings are shut, the natural way of bearing them being spread, with the top pointing to the chief of the angle.
Humbling, depressing, bringing low.

Abhor.pronunciation: ABHOR', [L abhorreo, of ab and horreo, to set up bristles, shiver or shake; to look terrible.]

definitions.text:
1. To hate extremely, with contempt; to lothe, detest abominate.
2. To despise neglect. Psalms 22:24. Amos 6:8.
3. To cast off reject. Psalm 89:38.
Hated extremely, detested.
Having great aversion, detesting. As a noun, it is used Isaiah lxvi, for the object of hatred - 'An abhorring to all flesh.'

Abib.pronunciation: A'BIB, [Heb. swelling, protuberant. To produce the first or early fruit; a full grown ear of corn.]

definitions.text: The first month of the Jewish ecclesiastical year, called also Nisan. It begins at the spring equinox, and answers to the latter part of March and beginning of April. Its name is derived from the full growth of wheat in Egypt, which took place anciently, as it does now, at that season.

1. Very odiously; detestably; sinfully. 1 Kings 21:26.
2. In vulgar language, extremely, excessively.

Abounding
Having great plenty; being great plenty, being very prevalent; generally prevailing.
Increase.

1. Kind reception; receiving with favor or approbation. This is saying worthy of all acceptation 1 Timothy 1:15.
2. state of being acceptable; favorable regard. Some things are of great dignity and acceptation with GodBut in this sense acceptableness is more generally used.
3. the meaning or sense in which word or expression is understood, or generally received; as term is to be used according to its usual acceptation
4. Reception in general. obsolete
One who accuses or blames; an officer who prefers an accusation against another for some offense, in the name of the government, before a tribunal that has cognizance of the offense.

field said to have laid south of Jerusalem, the same as the potters field, purchased with the bribe which Judas took for betraying his master, and therefore called the field of blood. It was appropriated to the interment of strangers.

1. The scald head, a disease forming scaly eruptions, supposed to be a critical evacuation of acrimonious humors; a species of herpes.
2. In mythology, the God of flies, said to have been worshipped by the Cyreneans, to avoid being vexed by those insects.
Making known to; giving notice, or information to.

Hebrew month, answering to the latter part of February, and the beginning of March, the 12th of the sacred and 6th of the civil year; so named to become glorious, from the exuberance of vegetation, in that month, in Egypt and Palestine.

Adjure.pronunciation: ADJU'RE, [Latin adjuro, to swear solemnly, or compel one to swear; from ad and juro, to swear.]

definitions.text:
1. To charge, bind or command on oath, or under the penalty of a curse.
Joshua adjured them at that time, saying, cursed be the man before the Lord, that riseth up and buildeth this city of Jericho. Joshua 6:26.
2. To charge earnestly and solemnly, on pain of God's wrath.
I adjure thee by the living God. Matthew 26:63, Acts 19:13.
3. To conjure; to charge, urge or summon with solemnity.
The magistrates adjured by all the bonds of civil duty.
Ye sacred stars, be all of you adjured.
The Commissioners adjured them not to let pass so favorable an opportunity of securing their liberties.
Charged on oath, or with a denunciation of God's wrath; solemnly urged.

Admonish.pronunciation: ADMON'ISH, [Latin admoneo, ad and moneo, to teach, warn, admonish ]

definitions.text:
1. To warn or notify of a fault; to reprove with mildness.
Count him not as an enemy, but admonish him as a brother. 2 Thessalonians 3:15.
2. To counsel against wrong practices; to caution or advise.
Admonish one another in psalms and hymns. Colossians 3:16.
3. To instruct or direct.
Moses was admonished of God, when he was about to make the tabernacle. Hebrews 8:5.
4. In ecclesiastical affairs, to reprove a member of the church for a fault, either publicly or privately; the first step of church discipline. It followed by of, or against; as, to admonish of a fault committed, or against committing a fault. It has a like use in colleges.
Reproving; warning; counseling; directing.

1. man guilty of adultery; a man who has sexual commerce with any married woman, except his wife. [See Adultery.
2. In scripture, an idolator. Ezekiel 23:37.
3. An apostate from the true faith, or one who violates his covenant engagements; a very wicked person. Jeremiah 9:2 and 23.
4. One devoted to earthly things. James 4:4.]
married woman guilty of incontinence.
Benefitted; promoted.
Put to hazard; ventured; risked.

1. Counsel; information; circumspection.
2. Consultation.
The action standing continued nisi for advisement (Massachusetts Reports)
First occurrence in the Bible(KJV): 1 Chronicles 12:19

1. Disposed; having an affection of heart. Be ye kindly affectioned to another. Romans 12:10.
2. Affected; conceited. obsolete

Afflict.pronunciation: AFFLICT', [Latin affligo, afflicto, of ad and figo, to strike; eng. flog; Gr. to strike; , Latin plaga, a stroke. Hence, eng. flail, g being suppressed; Latin flagellum. See Flog.]

definitions.text:
1. To give to the body or mind pain which is continued or of some permanence; to grieve, or distress; as, one is afflicted with the gout, or with melancholy, or with losses and misfortunes.
They afflict thy heritage, O Lord. Psalms 94:5.
2. To trouble; to harass; to distress.

Affright
affri'te. [See Fright.] To impress with sudden fear; to frighten; to terrify or alarm. It expresses a stronger impression than fear or apprehend, and perhaps less than terror.
Sudden or great fear; terror; also, the cause of terror; a frightful object.
Suddenly alarmed with fear; terrified; followed by at or with, more generally by at; as, affrighted at the cry of fire.

Aforehand
[afore hand.]
1. In time previous; by previous provision; as, he is ready aforehand She is come aforehand to anoint my body. Mark 14:8.
2.

Prepared; previously provided; as, to be aforehand in business. Hence in popular language, amply provided; well supplied with the means of living; having means beyond the requirements of necessity; moderately wealthy. The word is popularly changed into aforehanded, beforehanded, rather forehanded; as, forehanded farmer.
In past; in a former time.
or 'AFTERWARDS, [See Ward.] In later or subsequent time.

Agate.pronunciation: AGA'TE, [a and gate.] [Gr. so called, says Pliny, 34, 10, because found near a river of that name in Sicily. So also Solinus and Isidore. But Bochart, with more probability, deduces it from the Punic and Hebrew, and with a different prefix, spotted. The word is used, Genesis 30:32 and 33, to describe the speckled and spotted cattle of Laban and Jacob.]A

definitions.text:
Agate
[a and gate.] On the way; going. obsolete
AG'ATE,

A class of siliceous, semi-pellucid gems of many varieties, consisting of quartz-crystal, flint, horn-stone, chalcedony, amethyst, jasper, cornealian, heliotrope, and jade, in various combinations, variegated with dots, zones, filaments, ramifications, arborizations, and various figures. Agates seem to have been formed by successive layers of siliceous earth, on the sides of cavities which they now fill entirely or in part.
They are esteemed the least valuable of the precious stones. Even in Pliny's time, they were in little estimation. They are found in rocks, in the form of fragments, in nodules, in small rounded lumps, rarely in stalactites. Their colors are various. They are used for rings, seals, cups, beads, boxes and handles of small utensils.
AG'ATE,

An instrument used by goldwire drawers, so called from the agate in the middle of it.
First occurrence in the Bible(KJV): Exodus 28:19
agawn; [See ago and Gone.] Ago; past; since.[Nearly obsolete ]

Ague
a'gu,
1. The cold fit which precedes a fever, or a paroxysm of fever in intermittents. It is accompanied with shivering.
2. Chilliness; a chill, or state of shaking with cold, though in health.
3. It is used for a periodical fever, an intermittent, whether quotidian, tertian, or quartan. In this case, the word, which signifies the preceding cold fit, is used for the disease.
To cause a shivering in; to strike with a cold fit.

In scripture, a tree or wood about which the learned are not agreed. The most probably conjecture is that the word denotes gummy or resinous wood in general. The Vulgate translates it ligna thyina, and the Septuagint, wrought-wood; others, ebony, bravil or pine, and the Rabbins render it coral. It was used for musical instruments, stair cases, etc. The thyinum is the citron tree, from Mauritania, much esteemed by the ancients for its fragrance and beauty. The almug, almugim, or algumin, or simply gummin is most probably a gummy wood, and perhaps may be the Shittim, often mentioned in Scripture. See 1 Kings 10:11.

In scripture a tree or wood.
First occurrence in the Bible(KJV): 1 Kings 10:11

noun, in medicine, is the inspissated juice of the aloe. The juice is collected from the leaves, which are cut and put in a tub, and when a large quantity is procured, it is boiled to a suitable consistence; or it is exposed to the sun, till all the fluid part is exhaled. There are several kinds sold in the shops; as the socotrine aloes from Socotora, an isle in the Indian ocean; the hepatic or common Barbadoes aloes; and the fetid or caballine aloes
ALOES is a stimulating stomachic purgative; when taken in small doses, it is useful for people of a lax habit and sedentary life.
or AL'WAYS, [all and way]
An ambush; which see.

Amerce
amers'. [A

formed from a for on or at, from Latin merces, reward.]
1. To inflict a penalty at mercy; to punish by a pecuniary penalty, the amount of which is not fixed by law, but left to the discretion or mercy of the court; as, the court amerced the criminal in the sum of one hundred dollars.
2. To inflict a pecuniary penalty; to punish in general. Milton uses of after amerce; 'Millions of spirits amerced of heaven; ' but this use seems to be a poetic license.

Ammonite.pronunciation: AM'MONITE, [Cornu ammonis, from Jupiter Ammon, whose statues were represented with ram's horns.]

definitions.text: Serpent-stone, or cornu ammonis, a fossil shell, curved into a spiral, like a ram's horn; of various sizes, from the smallest grains to three feet in diameter. This fossil is found in stratums of limestone and clay, and in argillaceous iron ore. It is smooth or ridged; the ridges strait, crooked or undulated.

Anise
an'nis. [Latin anisum; Gr.] annual plant, placed by Linne under the genus Pimpinella. It grows naturally in Egypt, and cultivated in Spain and Malta, whence the seeds are imported. The stalk rises a foot and a half high, dividing into slender branches, garnished with narrow leaves, cut into three or four narrow segments. The branches terminate in large loose umbels, composed of smaller umbels or rays, on long footstalks. The flowers are small and of a yellowish white; the seeds oblong and swelling. anise seeds have an aromatic smell, and a pleasant warm taste; they are useful in warming the stomach and expelling wind. SEED,
The seed of anise

1. To pour oil upon; to smear or rub over with oil or unctuous substances; also to spread over, as oil. We say, the man anoints another, or the oil anoints him.
2. To consecrate by unction, or the use of oil. Thou shalt anoint the altar, and sanctify it. Exodus 29:7.
3. To smear or daub. He anointed the eyes of the blind man with clay. John 9:6.
4. To prepare, allusion to the consecrating use of oil. the shield. Isaiah 21:5. To anoint the head with oil, Psalms 23:5. seems to signify to communicate the consolations of the Holy Spirit. The use of oil consecrations, was of high antiquity. Kings, prophets and priests were set apart or consecrated to their offices by the use of oil. Hence the peculiar application of the term anointed to Jesus Christ.

Anointing
Smearing with oil; pouring on oil, or other oleaginous substance; consecrating.
The act of smearing with oil; a consecrating.

1. Quickly; without intermission:soon; immediately. The same is he that heareth the word, and anon with joy receiveth it. Matthew 13:20.
2. Sometimes; now and then; at other times; accompanied with ever, ever and anon
The destroyer; a name used Revelation 9:11, for the angel of the bottomless pit, answering to the Hebrew Abaddon.
Quieted; calmed; stilled; pacified.

Appertain.pronunciation: APPERTA'IN, [Latin ad and pertineo, to pertain, of per and teneo, to hold. Pertineo is to reach to, to extend to, hence to belong. See Tenant.]To

definitions.text:
To belong, whether by right, nature or appointment.
Give it to him to whom it appertaineth. Leviticus 6:5.
[See Pertain.]
In ornithology, the eagle. Also northern constellation containing, according to the British catalogue, 71 stars.

1. An of the highest order; occupying the eighth rank in the celestiai hierarchy.
2. The name of several plants, as the dead-nettle, or lamium; species of melittis; and the galeopsis or hedge-nettle.
fixed star of the first magnitude, in the constellation of Bootes.
The termination of many English words. We observe it in Goddard, a divine temper; Giffard, a disposition to give, liberality; Bernard, flial affection; standard, drunkard, dotard, etc.
member of the Areopagus, which see. Acts 17:34.

sovereign tribunal at Athens, famous for the justice and impartiality of its decisions. It was originally held on hill in the city; but afterward removed to the Royal Portico, an open square, where the judges sat in the open air, inclosed by cord. Their sessions were in the night, that they might not be diverted by objects of sight, or influenced by the presence and action of the speakers. By law of Solon, no person could be member of this tribunal, until he had been archon or chief magistrate. This court took cognizance of high crimes, impiety and immorality, and watched over the laws and the public treasury.

Arkite
term used by Bryant to denote one of the persons who were preserved in the ark; or who, according to pagan fables, belonged to the ark.'ARKITE,
Belonging to the ark.
Set in order, or in lines; arranged in order for attack or defense; dressed; adorned by dress; impaneled, as jury; enveloped.
Arrogance. [This orthography is less usual.]

Artificer.pronunciation: ARTIF'ICER, [Latin artifex, from ars, and facio.]

definitions.text:
1. An artist; a mechanic or manufacturer; one whose occupation requires skill or knowledge of a particular kind; as a silversmith, or sadler.
2. One who makes or contrives; an inventor; as an artificer of fraud or lies.
3. cunning, or artful fellow. [Not used.]
plant.
Trying by some standard; examining by experiment, as metals; proving; attempting.

athrust'. [a and thirst. See Thirst.]
1. Thirsty; wanting drink.
2. Having a keen appetite or desire. He had a soul athirst for knowledge.
Attentive. 2 Chronicles 6:40.
Satisfied by the punishment of the offender; vindicated; punished.
One who avenges or vindicates; a vindicator; a revenger.
Executing vengeance; taking satisfaction for an injury by the punishment of the offender; vindicating.
Affirmed; maintained; called in to support.
An idle talker; an irrational prattler; teller of secrets.

Babylonish
Pertaining to Babylon, the capital of the ancient kingdom of Babylonia, or to the kingdom. The city stood the river Frat, or Euphrates, and it supposed, the spot where the tower of Babel was founded.
2. Like the language of Babel; mixed; confused. BABYLO'NIAN,
An inhabitant of Babylonia. In ancient writers, an astrologer, as the Chaldeans were remarkable for the study of astrology.
The act of slandering the absent; secret calumny. 2 Corinthians 12:20.

An apostate; one who falls from the faith and practice of religion. Proverbs 14:14.
2. One who neglects his vows of obedience and falls into sin.

6.

To administer the sacrament of baptism to; to christen. By some denominations of christians, baptism is performed by plunging, or immersing the whole body in water, and this is done to none but adults. More generally the ceremony is performed by sprinkling water on the face of person, whether an infant or an adult, and in the case of an infant, by giving him name, in the name of the Father, Son and Holy Spirit, which is called Christening.
Administering baptism to; christening.

The quality of not producing its kind; want of the power of conception; applied to animals.
2. Unfruitfulness; sterility, infertility. The quality of not producing at all, or in small quantities; as the barrenness of soil.
3. Want of invention; want of the power of producing any thing new; applied to the mind.
4. Want of matter; scantiness; as the barrenness of cause.
5. Defect of emotion, sensibility or fervency; as the barrenness of devotion.

Battlement.pronunciation: BAT'TLEMENT, [This is said to have been bastillement, from bastille, a fortification.]

definitions.text: wall raised on building with openings or embrasures, or theembrasure itself.

Bdellium.pronunciation: BDEL'LIUM, [ Bochard and Parkhurst translate it, pearl. Genesis 2:12. But it is doubtful whether the bdellium of the scriptures is that now used.]

definitions.text: A gummy resinous juice, produced by a tree in the East Indies, of which we have no satisfactory account. It is brought from the E. Indies and from Arabia, in pieces of different sizes and figures, externally of a dark reddish brown, internally, clear and not unlike to glue. To the taste, it is slightly bitterish and pungent; its odor is agreeable. In the mouth, it becomes soft and sticks to the teeth; on a red hot iron, it readily catches flame and burns with a crackling noise. It is used as a perfume and a medicine, being a weak deobstruent.

Beautify
bu'tify. [beauty and Latin facio.] To make or render beautiful; to adorn; to deck; to grace; to add beauty to; to embellish.
bu'tify. To become beautiful; to advance in beauty.
plural of beef. Cattle; quadrupeds of the bovine genus, called in England, black cattle.
Beget
begot, begat;

begot, begotten.
1. To procreate, as a father or sire; to generate; as, to beget a son.
2. To produce, as an effect; to cause to exist; to generate; as, luxury begets vice.

Beggarly
Mean; poor; in the condition of beggar; extremely indigent.
Meanly; indigently; despicable.
of get. Procreated; generated.

begi'le. [be and guile.] To delude; to deceive; to impose on by artifice or craft. The serpent beguiled me and did eat Genesis 3:13.
1. To elude by craft. When misery could beguile the tyrant's rage.
2. To elude any thing disagreeable by amusement, or other means; to pass pleasingly; to amuse; as, to beguile the tedious day with sleep.
Deluded; imposed on; misled by craft; eluded by stratagem; passed pleasingly.
Deluding; deceiving by craft; eluding by artifice, amusing.

The latter opinion is most probably. [See Hippopotamus.] The original word in Arabic signifies a brute or beast in general, especially a quadruped.
First Occurrence in the Bible(KJV): Job 40:15

Fixing the eyes upon; looking on; seeing.
1. Fixing the attention; regarding with attention.
2. Obligation. [Not used.]
3. Obliged. Bacon on Love. A mistaken use of the word for beholden.

Belch
[Eng. bulge, bilge, bulk.]
1. To throw or eject wind from the stomach with violence.
2. To eject violently from a deep hollow place, as, a volcano belches flames and lava.

The act of throwing out from the stomach, or from a hollow place; eructation.
1. A cant name for malt liquor.
As noun, unprofitableness; wickedness. As an adjective, worthless; wicked. In collective sense, wicked men.
To lament; to bewail; to express sorrow for; as, to bemoan the loss of son.
Lamented; bewailed.
Bereave
bereaved, bereft:

bereaved, bereft.
1. To deprive; to strip; to make destitute; with of before the thing taken away.
Me have ye bereaved of my children. Genesis 42:36.
It is sometimes used without of, and is particularly applied to express the loss of friends by death.
2. To take away from.
Beseech
and

besought. To entreat; to supplicate; to implore; to ask or pray with urgency; followed by a person; as, 'I Paul beseech you by the meekness of Christ, ', 2 Corinthians 10:1; or by a thing; as, I beseech your patience.
Entreating.

1. To lay to; to beleaguer; to beset, or surround with armed forces, for the purpose of compelling to surrender, either by famine or by violent attacks; as, to besiege a castle or city.
2. To beset; to throng round.

Besom
as z. A broom; a brush of twigs for sweeping. I will sweep it with the besom of destruction, saith the Lord of Hosts. Isaiah 14:23.
To sweep, as with a besom Rolls back all Greece, and besoms wide the plain.

and

bestur' [be and stir.] To put into brisk or vigorous action; to move with life and vigor; usually with the reciprocal pronoun; as, rise and bestir yourselves.

Betroth.pronunciation: BETROTH', [be and troth, truth, faith. See Truth, and Troth.]

definitions.text:
1. To contract to any one, in order to a future marriage; to promise or pledge one to be the future spouse of another; to affiance; used of either sex. 'The father betroths his daughter.'
2. To contract with one for a future spouse; to espouse; as, a man betroths a lady.
3. To nominate to a bishopric, in order to consecration.

preposition
1. Between; in the space that separates two persons or things; as, betwixt two oaks.
2. Passing between; from one to another, noting intercourse. [See Between.]

Bewail
[be and wail.] To bemoan; to lament; to express sorrow for. It expresses deep sorrow; as, to bewail the loss of child. The true penitent bewails his ingratitude to God.
To express grief.
Lamented; bemoaned.

beray. To disclose perfidiously; to betray; to show or make visible.
Thy speech bewrayeth thee. Matthew 26:73.
[This word is nearly antiquated.]

Bier.pronunciation: BIER, [Latin feretrum, from fero. See Bear.]A

definitions.text:
A carriage or frame of wood for conveying dead human bodies to the grave.
First Occurrence in the Bible(KJV): 2 Samuel 3:31

Bittern
A fowl of the grallic order, the Ardea stellaris, a native of Europe. This fowl has long legs and neck, and stalks among reeds and sedge, feeding upon fish. It makes a singular noise, called by Dryden bumping, and by Goldsmith booming.

In salt works, the brine remaining after the salt is concreted. This being laded off, and the salt taken out of the pan, is returned, and being again boiled, yields more salt. It is used in the preparation of Epsom salt, the sulphate of magnesia, and of Glauber's salt, the sulphate of soda.

Blaspheme.pronunciation: BLASPHE'ME, [Gr. The first syllable is the same as in blame, blasme, denoting injury; Latin loedo, loesus; The last syllable is the Gr., to speak.]

definitions.text:
Blaspheme
[Gr. The first syllable is the same in blame, blasme, denoting injury; Latin loedo, loesus; The last syllable is the Gr., to speak.]
1. To speak of the Supreme Being in terms of impious irreverence; to revile or speak reproachfully of God, or the Holy Spirit. 1 Kings 21:10. Mark 3:28.
2. To speak evil of; to utter abuse or calumny against; to speak reproachfully of.
BLASPHE'ME,

To utter blasphemy.
He that shall blaspheme against the Holy Spirit shall not be forgiven. Mark 3:29.
1. To arrogate the prerogatives of God.
This man blasphemeth. Who can forgive sins but God? Math.
9. Mark 2:7.
One who blasphemes; one who speaks of God in impious and irreverent terms. 1 Timothy 1:13.
Uttering impious or reproachful words concerning God.
Impiously; with impious irreverence to God.

Bleating
or
Crying as sheep.
The cry of sheep.

Happiness; felicity; heavenly joys; the favor of God.
1. Sanctity.
Having the eyes covered; hindered from seeing.

female slave, or one bound to service without wages, in opposition to hired servant.
slave, or one bound to service without wages. In old English law, villain, or tenant in villenage.
slave; one who is subjected to the authority of another, or whose person and liberty are restrained.
The condition of a bond-servant; slavery.

Botch
[Eng.patch.]

Liberally; largely; in a bountiful manner.
The quality of being bountiful; liberality in the bestowment of gifts and favors.

brazn. Made of brass. [See Brass and Brazen.]
noisy fellow; wrangler.

A kiln, or furnace, in which bricks are baked or burnt, or a pile of bricks, laid loose, with arches underneath to receive the wood or fuel.
The nuptial apartment. Matthew 9:15.
