Buckler
A kind of shield, or piece of defensive armor, anciently used in war. It was composed of wood, or wickers woven together, covered with skin or leather, fortified with plates of brass or other metal, and worn on the left arm. On the middle was an umbo, boss or prominence, very useful in causing stones and darts to glance off. The buckler often was four feet long, and covered the whole body.
To support; to defend. [Not used.]
Put forth in buds; inoculated.
The common flounder.
A large kind of rush, growing in wet land or water, and without knots, says Johnson, but Dryden calls it, the knotty bulrush It is not a technical word.

A dry measure, containing eight gallons, or four pecks. The standard English bushel by Stat. 12 . Henry VII., contains eight gallons of wheat, each gallon eight pounds of wheat, troy weight, the pound, twelve ounces troy, the ounce, twenty sterlings, and the sterling, thirty two grains of wheat growing in the middle of the ear. The contents are 2145. 6 solid inches, equivalent to 1131 ounces and 14 pennyweights troy. The English bushel is used also in the U. States. signifies both the quantity or capacity, and the vessel which will contain the quantity.
1. In popular language, a large quantity indefinitely.
2. The circle of iron in the nave of a wheel; in America, called a box. [See Bush.]
The office of a butler. Genesis 40:21.

1. The generic name of the Indian cane, called also rotang. It is without branches, has crown at the top, and is beset with spines.
2. In antiquity, pipe or fistula, wind instrument, made of reed or oaten stalk.
3. rush or reed used anciently as pen to write on parchment or papyrus.
4. sort of reed, or sweet-scented cane, used by the Jews as perfume. It is knotty root, reddish without and white within, and filled with spungy substance. It has an aromatic smell.
5. The sweet flag, called by Linne Acorus.
large kettle or boiler, of copper, or other metal, furnished with movable handle or bail, with which to hang it chimney hook.

1. place of skulls; particularly, the place where Christ was crucified, on small hill west of Jerusalem. In catholic countries, kind of chapel raised on hillock near city, as place of devotion, in memory of the place where our Savior suffered.
2. In heraldry, cross so called, set upon steps, resembling the cross on which our Savior was crucified.

1. To bring forth young, as cow.
2. In metaphorical sense, and sometimes by way of reproach, as when applied to the human race, to bring forth; to produce.

Canker
1. disease incident to trees, which causes the bark to rot and fall.
2. popular name of certain small eroding ulcers in the mouth, particularly of children. They are generally covered with whitish slough.
3. virulent, corroding ulcer; or any thing that corrodes, corrupts or destroys. Sacrilege may prove eating canker And their word will eat as doth canker Tim.
2.
4. An eating, corroding, virulent humor; corrosion.
5. kind of rose, the dog rose.
6. In farriery, running thrush of the worst kind; disease in horses feet, discharging fetid matter from the cleft in the middle of the frog.
To grow corrupt; to decay, or waste away by means of any noxious cause; to grow rusty, or to be oxydized, as metal.

Cankered
1. Corrupted.
2.
Crabbed; uncivil.
These words are usually united, but perhaps without good reason; canst and are never united.

1. Anxiety; solicitude. Drink thy water with trembling and with carefulness Ezekiel 12:18.
2. Heedfulness; caution; vigilance, in guarding against evil, and providing for safety.
In carnal manner; according to the flesh; in manner to gratify the flesh or sensual desire. Leviticus 18:20. Romans 8:6.
The wrist, but not an English word.

genus of plants of many species, among which are the fistula, or purging cassia and the senna. The former is native of Egypt and both Indies; the latter is native of Persia, Syria and Arabia. The latter is shrubby plant, the leaves of which are much used in medicine. The purging cassia is the pulp of the pods, and is gentle laxative. is also the name of species of Laurus, the bark of which usually passes under the name of cinnamon, differing from real cinnamon chiefly in the strength of its qualities. From plant of this kind was extracted an aromatic oil, used perfume by the Jews.

Castaway
That which is thrown away. person abandoned by God, unworthy of his favor; reprobate. 1 Corinthians 9:27.
Rejected; useless; of no value.

1. In anatomy, membrane in the abdomen, covering the greatest part of the lower intestines, called from its structure, reticuluim, net, but more generally, the omentum; also, little membrane sometimes encompassing the head of child when born.
2. kind of net in which females inclose their hair; the hinder part of cap.
3. Any kind of net.

1. Having no cause, or producing agent.
2. Without just ground, reason or motive; as causeless hatred; causeless fear. Proverbs 26:2.
A vase or pan in which incense is burned. Among the Jews, a kind of chafing-dish, covered by a dome, and suspended by a chain, used to offer perfumes in sacrifices.
Heated or fretted by rubbing; worn by friction.

subspecies of quartz, mineral called also white agate, resembling milk diluted with water, and more or less clouded or opake, with veins, circles and spots. It is used in jewelry. The varieties of chalcedony are common chalcedony heliotrope, chrysoprase, plasma, onyx, sard and sardonyx.
An inhabitant of Chaldea.
Wanton, lewd, immodest behavior. Romans 13:13.

1. The upper part or capital of column or pillar; word used in the scriptures. [See Capital.]
2. That which is delivered by the mouth of the justice in his charge to the inquest.

1. To correct by punishment; to punish; to inflict pain for the purpose of reclaiming an offender; as, to chasten son with rod. I will chasten him with the rod of men. 2 Samuel 7:14.
2. To afflict by other means. As many I love I rebuke and chasten Revelation 3:19.
3. To purify from errors or faults.
Corrected; punished; afflicted for correction.

Chastening
Correcting; afflicting for correction.
CHASTENING,

Correction; punishment for the purpose of reclaiming.
No chastening for the present seemeth to be joyous but grievous. Hebrews 12:11.

1. To correct by punishing; to punish; to inflict pain by stripes, or in other manner, for the purpose of punishing an offender and recalling him to his duty. will chastise you seven times for your sins. Leviticus 26:28.
2. To reduce to order or obedience; to restrain; to awe; to repress. The gay social sense, By decency chastisd.
3. To correct; to purify by expunging faults; as, to chastise poem.
Punished; corrected.

Correction; punishment; pain inflicted for punishment and correction, either by stripes or otherwise. Shall so much dishonour my fair stars, On equal terms to give him chastisement have borne chastisement will not offend any more. Job 34:31. The chastisement of our peace, in Scripture, was the pain which Christ suffered to purchase our peace and reconciliation to God. Isaiah 53:5.

plural Cherubs, but the Hebrew plural cherubim is also used. A figure composed of various creatures, as a man, an ox, an eagle or lion. The first mention of cherubs is in Genesis 3:24, where the figure is not described, but their office was, with a flaming sword, to keep or guard the way of the tree of life. The two cherubs which Moses was commanded to make at the ends of the Mercy seat, were to be of beaten work of gold; and their wings were to extend over the Mercy seat, their faces towards each other, and between them was the residence of the Deity. Exodus 15:1. The cherubs, in Ezekiels vision, had each four heads or faces, the hands of a man and wings. The four faces were, the face of a bull, that of a man, that of a lion, and that of an eagle. They had the likeness of a man. Ezekiel 4:1, and
10. In 2 Samuel 22:11, and Psalms 18:10, Jehovah is represented as riding on a cherub and flying on the wings of the wind. In the celestial hierarchy, cherubs are represented as spirits next in order to seraphs. The hieroglyphical and emblematical figures embroidered on the vails of the tabernacle are called cherubs of curious or skilful work. Exodus 26:1.

Chide
1. To scold at; to reprove; to utter words in anger, or by way of disapprobation; to rebuke; as, to chide one for his faults.
2. To blame; to reproach; as, to chide folly or negligence. To chide from or chide away, is to drive away by scolding or reproof.

1. To scold; to clamor; to find fault; to contend in words of anger; sometimes followed by with. The people did chide with Moses. Exodus 17:2.
2. To quarrel.
3. To make a rough, clamorous, roaring noise; as the chiding flood.
Murmur; gentle noise.

Chiding
Scolding; clamoring; rebuking; making a harsh or continued noise.
A scolding or clamoring; rebuke; reproof.
The ninth month of the Jewish year, answering to a part of November and a part of December, in the modern division of the year.
the old of chide, which see.

1. The bile. By the superabundance of this fluid, anger was formerly supposed to be produced; or perhaps the opinion was that the bile caused the inflamed appearance of the face in anger. Hence,
2. Anger; wrath; irritation of the passions.
Cholera Morbus, a sudden evacuation of bile, both upwards and downwards.
First occurrence in the Bible(KJV): Daniel 8:7

A mineral, called by Hauy and Brongniart, peridote and by Jameson, prismatic chrysolite Its prevailing color is some shade of green. It is harder than glass, but less hard than quartz; often transparent, sometimes only translucent. It occurs sometimes in crystals, sometimes in small amorphous masses or grains, and sometimes in rolled pieces.

1. A rude, surly, ill-bred man.
2. A rustic; a countryman, or laborer.
3. A miser; a niggard. Isaiah 32:5.

To cut off the prepuce or foreskin of males; a ceremony or rite in the Jewish and Mohammedan religions. The word applied also to a practice among some nations of performing a like operation upon females.
Cautiously; with watchfulness every way; with attention to guard against surprise or danger.
Speaking and repeating loud words; noisy; vociferous; loud; turbulent.
Of cleave.

1. Freedom from dirt, filth, and foreign matter; neatness.
2. Freedom from infection or foul disease.
3. Exactness; purity; justness; correctness; used of language or style; as, cleanness of expression.
4. Purity; innocence. In scripture, cleanness of hands denotes innocence. cleanness of teeth denotes want of provisions. Amos 4:6.

1. Freedom from foul or extraneous matter; purity; as the clearness of water, or other liquor.
2. Freedom from obstruction or incumbrance; as the clearness of the ground.
3. Freedom from fogs or clouds; openness; as the clearness of the sky. It generally expresses less than brightness or splendor. Exodus 24:10.
4. Distinctness; perspicuity; luminousness; as the clearness of reason, of views, of arguments, of explanations.
5. Plainness, or plain dealing; sincerity; honesty; fairness; candor.
6. Freedom from imputation of ill.
7. Freedom from spots, or any thing that disfigures; as the clearness of the skin.

Cleave
1. To stick; to adhere; to hold to. My bones cleave to my skin. Psalms 102:5. Let my tongue cleave to the roof of my mouth. Psalms 137:6. to that which is good. Romans 12:9.
2. To unite aptly; to fit; to sit well on.
3. To unite or be united closely in interest or affection; to adhere with strong attachment. man shall father and mother, and cleave to his wife. Genesis 2:24. Math.
19. to Jehovah your God. Joshua 23:8.

1. To part or divide by force; to split or rive; to open or serve the cohering parts of body, by cutting or by the application of force; as, to cleave wood; to cleave rock; to cleave the flood. Psa
74.
2. To part or open naturally. Every beast that cleaveth the cleft into two claws. Deuteronomy 14:1.
To part; to open; to crack; to separate, as parts of cohering bodies; as, the ground cleaves by frost. The mount of Olives shall cleave in the midst thereof. Zechariah 14:4.
Split; rived; divided.

Cloke
1. A loose outer garment worn over other clothes both by men and women. He was clad with zeal as a cloke Isaiah 59:17.
2. A cover; that which conceals; a disguise or pretext; an excuse; a fair pretense. Not using your liberty for a cloke of maliciousness. 1 Peter 2:16. They have no cloke for their sin. John 15:22.

1. To cover with a cloke
2. To hide; to conceal; to use a false covering.
Patched; mended clumsily; covered with a clout.
of cleave. Divided; parted; pronounced clovn.
serpent imagined to proceed from cocks egg.

Cockle
A plant or weed that grows among corn, the cornrose, a species of Agrostemma. It is also applied to the Lolium or darnel.

1. A small testaceous shell; or rather a genus of shells, the Cardium. The general characteristics are; shells nearly equilateral and equivalvular; hinge with two small teeth, one on each side near the beak, and two larger remote lateral teeth, one on each side; prominent ribs running from the hinge to the edge of the valve.
2. A mineral; a name given by the Cornish miners to shirl or shorl.
3. A young cock.
or t. To contract into wrinkles; to shrink, pucker, or wrinkle, as cloth.

1. A chest or trunk; and as a chest is customarily used for keeping money, hence,
2. A chest money; a treasure.
3. In architecture, a square depression or sinking in each interval between the modillions the Corinthian cornice, ordinarily filled with a rose, a pomegranate or other enrichment.
4. In fortification, a hollow lodgment across a dry moat, from 6 to 7 feet deep and from 16 to 18 broad; the upper part made pieces timber, raised two feet above the level the moat; which little elevation has hurdles laden with earth for its covering, and serves as a parapet with embrasures. It is raised by the besieged to repulse besiegers when they endeavor to pass the ditch.

That which is becoming, fit or suitable, form or manner. comeliness of person implies symmetry or due proportion of parts; comeliness of manner implies decorum and propriety. It signifies something less forcible than beauty, less elegant than grace, and less light than prettiness. A careless comeliness with comely care. He hath no form nor comeliness Isaiah 53:2.

1. One who administers comfort consolation; one who strengthens and supports the mind in distress danger. I looked comforters, but found none. Psalms 69:20. Miserable comforters are ye all. Job 16:2.
2. The title of the Holy Spirit, whom the Father will send in my name--he shall teach you all things. John 14:16.
Without comfort; without any thing to alleviate misfortune, distress. I will not leave you comfortless John 14:18.

Praising; representing favorably; committing, or delivering charge. Note: In imitation of the French, we are accustomed to use recommendation, etc., for commendation. But most instances, it is better to use the word without the prefix re. A letter of commendation, is the preferable phrase.

Communing
Conversing familiarly; having familiar intercourse.
Familiar converse; private intercourse.
Embraced; surrounded; inclosed; obtained; imagined.

1. Embracing; going round; inclosing; obtaining; accomplishing; imagining; intending.
2. In ship-building, incurvated; arched.

Literally, a cutting off. Hence, In scripture, the Jews or those who adhered to circumcision, which, after our Saviors death, was no longer a seal of the covenant, but a mere cutting of the flesh. Beware of dogs; beware of the concision Philippians 3:2.

Concubine.pronunciation: CONCUBINE, [Latin , to lie together, to lie down.]

definitions.text:
1. A woman who cohabits with a man, without the authority of a legal marriage; a woman kept for lewd purposes; a kept mistress.
2. A wife of inferior condition; a lawful wife, but not united to the man by the usual ceremonies, and of inferior condition. Such were Hagar and Keturah, the concubines of Abraham; and such concubines were allowed by the Roman laws.

Concupiscence.pronunciation: CONCUPISCENCE, [Latin , to covet or lust after, to desire or covet.]

definitions.text: Lust; unlawful or irregular desire of sexual pleasure. In a more general sense, the coveting of carnal things, or an irregular appetite for worldly good; inclination for unlawful enjoyments. We know even secret concupiscence to be sin. Sin, taking occasion by the commandment, wrought in me all manner of concupiscence Romans 7:8.

1. To from the privileges of superior rank or dignity, to do some act to an inferior, which strict justice or the ordinary rules of civility do not require. Hence, to submit or yield, as to an inferior, implying an occasional relinquishment of distinction.
Mind not high things, but condescend to men of low estate. Romans 12:16.
2. To recede from ones rights in negotiation, or common intercourse, to do some act, which strict justice does not require.
Spains mighty monarch, in gracious clemency does condescend these conditions, to become your friend.
3. To stoop or descend; to yield; to submit; implying a relinquishment of rank, or dignity of character, and sometimes a sinking into debasement.
Can they think me so broken, so debased, with corporal servitude, that my mind ever will condescend to such absurd commands?

Confederate.pronunciation: CONFEDERATE, [Latin] United in a league; allied by treaty; engaged in a confederacy. These were confederate with Abram. Genesis 14:13. Syria is confederate with Ephraim. Isaiah 7:2. CONFED'ERATE, noun One who is united with others in a league; a person or nation engaged in a confederacy; an ally. CONFED'ERATE, verb intransitive [Latin]

definitions.text:
Confederate
[Latin] United in league; allied by treaty; engaged in confederacy. These were confederate with Abram. Genesis 14:13. Syria is confederate with Ephraim. Isaiah 7:2. CONFED'ERATE,
One who is united with others in league; person or nation engaged in confederacy; an ally. CONFED'ERATE,

To unite in league; to join in mutual contract or covenant. By words men come to know one another's minds; by these they covenant and confederate The colonies of America confederated in 1775. Several States of Europe have sometimes confederated for mutual safety. CONFED'ERATE,
To unite in league; to ally. With these the Piercies them confederate

1. Correspondent; having the same similar external form, shape; like; resembling; as an edifice conformable to model draft.
2. Having the same similar manners, opinions moral qualities. The Gentiles were not made conformable to the Jews, in that which was to cease at the coming of Christ.
3. Agreeable; suitable; consistent; as, nature is conformable to herself.
4. Compliant; ready to follow directions; submissive; obsequious; peaceable; disposed to obey. I have been to you true and humble wife, At all time to your will conformable It is generally followed by to, but good writers have used with. In its etymological sense, that may be conformed, capable of being conformed, it seems not to be used.
Converted into ice, or solid mass, by the loss of heat or other process; concreted.

Consecrate.pronunciation: CONSECRATE, [Latin , to consecrate sacred. See Sacred.]

definitions.text:
Consecrate
[Latin to consecrate sacred. See Sacred.]
1. To make or declare to be sacred, by certain ceremonies or rites; to appropriate to sacred uses; to set apart, dedicate, or devote, to the service and worship of God; as, to consecrate church. Thou shalt consecrate Aaron and his sons. Exodus 29:9. All the silver, and gold, and vessels of brass and iron, are consecrated to the Lord. Joshua 6:19.
2. To canonize; to exalt to the rank of saint; to enroll among the gods, as Roman emperor.
3. To set apart and bless the elements in the eucharist.
4. To render venerable; to make respected; as, rules or principles consecrated by time.
Sacred; consecrated; devoted; dedicated. They were assembled in that consecrate place.[This word is now seldom used, unless in poetry.]
United in marriage.
One who consults, or asks counsel or information; as a consulter with familiar spirits. Deuteronomy 18:11.

Contemn.pronunciation: CONTEMN, [Latin , to despise; to drive away.]

definitions.text:
1. To despise; to consider and treat as mean and despicable; to scorn. In whose eyes a vile person is contemned. Psalms 15:4.
2. To slight; to neglect as unworthy of regard; to reject with disdain. Wherefore do the wicked contemn God. Psalms 10:13. They contemn the counsel of the Most High. Psalms 107:11.
Despised; scorned; slighted; neglected, or rejected with disdain.
On the contrary; oppositely; the other hand. Not rendering evil for evil, nor railing for railing; but contrariwise blessing. 1 Peter 3:9.

1. In Jewish antiquity, offering which had life; animal offered to God; in opposition to the mincha, which was offering without life. It is gift, corban by whatsoever thou mightest be profited by me; that is, I have devoted that to God which you ask of me, and it is no longer mine to give.
2. An alms-basket; vessel to receive gifts of charity; gift; alms; treasury of the church, where offerings are deposited.
3. Among Mohammedans, ceremony performed at the foot of mount Arrarat in Arabia, near Mecca. It consists in killing the number of sheep, and distributing them among the poor.

Cormorant.pronunciation: CORMORANT, [Cormorant is supposed to be corrupted from corvus marinus, sea raven. The Welsh also call the fowl morvran, sea crow.]

definitions.text:
1. The water raven, large fowl of the pelican kind; the head and neck are black; the coverts of the wings, the scapulars and the back are of deep green, edged with black and glossed with blue. The base of the lower mandible is covered with naked yellow skin, which extends under the chin and forms sort of pouch. This fowl occupies the cliffs by the sea, feeds on fish, and is extremely voracious.
2. glutton.

1. An instrument of music, in the nature of a trumpet, sounded by blowing with the mouth. It was of a winding shape like a horn; used in armies and on occasions of joy. David played before the Lord on cornets. 2 Samuel 6:5.
2. In modern usage, an officer of cavalry, who bears the ensign colors of a troop. He is the third officer in the company.
3. A company of cavalry; a troop of horse. [Not used.
4. The cornet of a horse [coronet[ is the lowest part of his pastern, that runs round the coffin and is distinguished by the hair that joins and covers the upper part of the hoof.
5. A little cap of paper in which retailers inclose small wares.
6. A scarf anciently worn by doctors.
7. A head dress.]
A for corn, for thrashing corn. Isaiah 21:10. Hosea  9:1.

Corruptible
1. That may be corrupted; that may become putrid; subject to decay and destruction. Our bodies are corruptible
2. That may be vitiated in qualities principles; susceptible of depravation. Manners are corruptible by evil example.
That which may decay and perish; the human body. This corruptible must put on incorruption. 1 Corinthians 15:53.

1. In a corrupt manner; with corruption; viciously; wickedly; without integrity. We have dealt very corruptly against thee. Nehemiah 1:7.
2. By bribery. A judgment was obtained corruptly
Expensiveness; great cost, or expense; sumptuousness. Revelations 18:19.

Couching
Lying down; laying down; lying close; involving; including; expressing; depressing a cataract.
The act of stooping or bowing.

Countervail.pronunciation: COUNTERVAIL, [counter and Latin , to avail or be strong.]

definitions.text:
Countervail
[counter and Latin to avail or be strong.] To act against with equal force, or power; to equal; to act with equivalent effect against any thing; to balance; to compensate. The profit will hardly countervail the inconveniences. Although the enemy could not countervail the kings damage. Esther 7:4.
Equal weight or strength; power or value sufficient to obviate any effect; equal weight or value; compensation; requital.
Pledged or promised by covenant.

Covet
1. To desire or wish for, with eagerness; to desire earnestly to obtain or possess; in a good sense. earnestly the best gifts. 1 Corinthians 12:31.
2. To desire inordinately; to desire that which it is unlawful to obtain or possess; in a bad sense. Thou shalt not covet thy neighbors house, wife or servant. Exodus 20:17.
To have an earnest desire. 1 Timothy 6:10.

1. Very desirous; eager obtain; in a good sense; as covetous of wisdom, virtue or learning.
2. Inordinately desirous; excessively eager obtain and possess; directed money or goods, avaricious. A bishop must not be covetous 1 Timothy 3:3.

1. A strong or inordinate desire of obtaining and possessing some supposed good; usually in a bad sense, and applied an inordinate desire of wealth or avarice. Out of the heart proceedeth covetousness Mark 7:22. Mortify your members--and covetousness which is idolatry. Colossians 3:5.
2. Strong desire; eagerness.
A contraction of cousin.

Artfulness; dexterity devising and effecting purpose; cunning; artifice; stratagem. He taketh the wise their own craftiness Job 5:13. Not walking craftiness nor handling the word of God deceitfully. 2 Corinthians 4:2.
Curling; frizzling.

1. To nail to a cross; to put to death by nailing the hands and feet to a cross or gibbet, sometimes anciently, by fastening a criminal to a tree, with cords.
But they cried, crucify him, crucify him. Luke 23:21.
2. In scriptural language, to subdue; to mortify; to destroy the power or ruling influence of.
They that are Christs have crucified the flesh, with the affections and lusts. Galatians 5:24.
3. To reject and despise.
They crucify to themselves the Son of God afresh. Hebrews 6:6.
To be crucified with Christ, is to become dead to the law and to sin, and to have indwelling corruption subdued. Galatians 2:20 and 6.
4. To vex or torment. [Not used.]
A small cup. Take with thee a cruse of honey. 1 Kings 14:3. In New England, it is used chiefly or wholly for a small bottle or vial for vinegar, called a vinegar-cruse.

Cubit.pronunciation: CUBIT, [Latin , the elbow; signifying a turn or corner; Gr.]

definitions.text:
1. In anatomy, the fore arm; the ulna, and bone of the arm from the elbow to the wrist.
2. In mensuration, the length of a mans arm from the elbow to the extremity of the middle finger. The cubit among the ancients was of a different length among different nations. Dr. Arbuthnot states the Roman cubit at seventeen inches and four tenths; the cubit of the scriptures at a little less than 22 inches; and the English cubit at 18 inches.
That which obstructs, retards, or renders motion or action difficult and toilsome; burden; encumbrance; hindrance; oppressive load; embarrassment.
An attendant of prince or at feast, who conveys wine or other liquors to the guests; an officer of the kings household. Nehemiah 1:11.
Coagulated; congealed.
in Saxon, signifies known, or famous. Hence, Cuthwin, a famous conqueror; Cuthred, a famous or knowing counselor; Cuthbert, known bright, or famous for skill.

1. musical instrument used by the ancients, hollow and made of brass, somewhat like kettle-drum; but the precise form is not ascertained.
2. mean instrument used by gypsies and vagrants, made of steel wire, in triangular form, on which are passed fie rings, which are touched and shifter along the triangle with an iron rod held in the left hand, while it is supported in the right by ring, to give it free motion.

1. That may be damned or condemned; deserving damnation; worthy of eternal punishment. More generally, that which subjects or renders liable to damnation. As damnable heresies. 2 Peter 2:1.
2. In low or ludicrous sense, odious, detestable, or pernicious.
Danced on the knee, or in the arms; fondled; amused by trifles or play.
Dusky; somewhat dark.

Daub
1. To smear with soft adhesive matter; to plaster; to cover with mud, slime, or other soft substance.
She took for him an ark of bulrushes, and daubed it with slime and with pitch. Exodus 2:3.
2. To paint coarsely.
If picture is daubed with many bright colors, the vulgar admire it. Watts.
3. To cover with something gross or specious; to disguise with an artificial covering.
So smooth he daubed his vice with show of virtue. Shak.
4. To lay or put on without taste; to deck awkwardly or ostentatiously, or to load with affected finery.
Let him be daubed with lace- Dryden.
5. To flatter grossly.
Conscience will not daub nor flatter. South.
DAUB,
To practice gross flattery; to play the hypocrite.

Daubing
Plastering; painting coarsely; disguising clumsily; decking ostentatiously; flattering grossly.
Plastering; coarse painting; gross flattery.
An umpire or arbiter; mediator. Neither is there any daysman betwixt us. Job 9:33.
The dawn; the beginning of the day, or first appearance of light. Whereby the dayspring from on high hath visited us. Luke I.

ded'ness.
1. Want of natural life or vital power, in an animal or plant; as the deadness of limb, of body, or of tree.
2. Want of animation; dullness; languor; as the deadness of the eye.
3. Want of warmth or ardor; coldness; frigidity; as the deadness of the affections.
4. State of being incapable of conception, according to the ordinary laws of nature. Romans 4:19.
5. Indifference; mortification of the natural desires; alienation of heart from temporal pleasures; as deadness to the world.

1. To reduce from higher to lower state or rank, in estimation. The drunkard debases himself and his character. Intemperance and debauchery debase men almost to level with beasts.
2. To reduce or lower in quality, purity, or value; to adulterate; as, to debase gold or silver by alloy.
3. To lower or degrade; to make mean or despicable. Religion should not be debased by frivolous disputes. Vicious habits debase the mind, well the character.
4. To sink in purity or elegance; to vitiate by meanness; as, to debase style by the use of vulgar words.

Decease.pronunciation: DECE'ASE, [Latin to depart or to withdraw.]

definitions.text:
Decease
[Latin to depart or to withdraw.] Literally, departure; hence, departure from this life; death; applied to human beings only. Moses and Elias, who appeared in glory, and spoke of his decease which he should accomplish at Jerusalem. Luke 9:31.
To depart from this life; to die. Gen. Washington deceased, December 14, 1799, in the 68th year of his age.

In a deceitful manner; fraudulently; with deceit; in a manner or with a view to deceive.
The sons of Jacob answered Shechem and Hamor his father deceitfully Genesis 34:13.

1. Tendency to mislead or deceive; as the deceitfulness of sin.
2. The quality of being fraudulent; as the deceitfulness of a man's practices.
3. The disposition to deceive; as, a man's deceitfulness may be habitual.

1. Liableness to be deceived.
2. Liableness to deceive. The deceivableness of unrighteousness. 2 Thessalonians 2:10.
One who deceives; one who leads into error; a cheat; an impostor. shall seem to my father as a deceiver Genesis 27:12.

1. Depth; remoteness from the surface in a descending line; interior distance from the surface; profundity.
And forthwith they sprung up, because they had no deepness of earth. Matthew 13:5
2. Craft; insidiousness.
Slandered; dishonored or injured by evil reports.

Defaming
Slandering; injuring the character by false reports.
Defamation; slander.

Defile
1. To make unclean; to render foul or dirty; in a general sense.
2. To make impure; to render turbid; as, the water or liquor is defiled.
3. To soil or sully; to tarnish; as reputation, etc. He is among the greatest prelates of the age, however his character may be defiled by dirty hands. They shall defile thy brightness. Ezekiel 28:7.
4. To pollute; to make ceremonially unclean. That which dieth of itself, he shall not eat, to defile himself therewith. Leviticus 22:8.
5. To corrupt chastity; to debauch; to violate; to tarnish the purity of character by lewdness. Schechem defiled Dinah. Genesis 34:2.
6. To taint, in a moral sense; to corrupt; to vitiate; to render impure with sin. not yourselves with the idols of Egypt. Ezekiel 20:7. He hath defiled the sanctuary of the Lord. Numbers 19:20.
To march off in a line, or by file; to off.
A narrow passage or way, in which troops may march only in a file, or with a narrow front; a long narrow pass, as between hills, etc.
Made dirty, or foul; polluted; soiled; corrupted; violated; vitiated.
Deprived of property or right by trick, artifice or deception; injured by the withholding of what is due.
The state of being delicate; tenderness; softness; effeminacy. Deuteronomy 28:56.
Very pleasing; delightful.

1. One who delivers; one who releases or rescues; a preserver. The Lord raised up a deliverer to Israel. Judges 3:9.
2. One who relates, or communicates.
Appointed as a substitute; appointed and sent with special authority to act for another.

To laugh at in contempt; to turn to ridicule or make sport of; to mock; to treat with scorn by laughter. The Pharisees also-derided him. Luke 16:14. Some, who adore Newton for his fluxions, deride him for his religion.

Descry
1. To espy; to explore; to examine by observation. The house of Joseph sent to descry Bethel. Judges 1:23.
2. To detect; to find out; to discover any thing concealed.
3. To see; to behold; to have a sight of from a distance; as, the seamen descried land.
4. To give notice of something suddenly discovered.
Discovery; thing descovered.

Full of spite; malicious; malignant; as a despiteful enemy.
Hater of God, despiteful, proud, boasters. Romans 1:30.
With despite; maliciously; contemptuously. Pray for them that despitefully use you. Matthew 5:44.
One who devours; he or that which eats, consumes or destroys; he that preys on.

Diadem.pronunciation: DIADEM, [Gr., to gird; to bind. Latin ]

definitions.text:
1. Anciently, head-band or fillet worn by kings as badge or royalty. It was made of silk, linen or wool, and tied round the temples and forehead, the ends being tied behind and let fall on the neck. It was usually white and plain; sometimes embroidered with gold, or set with pearls and precious stones.
2. In modern usage, the mark or badge of royalty, worn on the head; crown; and figuratively, empire; supreme power.
3. distinguished or principal ornament. diadem of beauty. Isaiah 28:5.

Digged
and
of dig.

To refuse permission, or not to permit; not to grant; not to make or suppose lawful; not to authorize; to disapprove. God disallows that Christians should conform to the immoral practices of the world. good man disallows every kind of profaneness.
2. To testify dislike or disapprobation; to refuse assent.
But if her father shall disallow her in the day that he heareth; not any of her vows, or of her bonds--shall stand. Numbers 30:5.
3. Not to approve; not to receive; to reject.
To whom coming, as to living stone, disallowed indeed of men, but chosen of God, and precious. 1 Peter 2:4.
4. Not to or admit as just; to reject; as, to disallow an account or charge.

Disannul.pronunciation: DISANNUL, [dis and annul. In this instance, the prefix dis is improperly used, and of no effect. But its use is well established.]

definitions.text: To annul; to make void; to deprive of authority or force; to nullify; to abolish; as, to disannul law or ordinance. Wilt thou also disannul my judgment? Job 40:8. Galatians 3:15.
Annulled; vacated; made void.
Making void; depriving of authority or binding force.

1. One who sees, discovers or distinguishes; an observer.
2. One who knows and judges; one who has the power of distinguishing. He was a great observer and discerner of mens natures and humors.
3. That which distinguishes; or that which causes to understand. The word of God quick and powerful--a discerner of the thoughts and intents of the heart. Hebrews 4:12.
Routed; defeated; overthrown.
Despised; contemned; scorned.

1. To change to a worse form; to mar external figure; to impair shape or form and render it less perfect and beautiful; as, the loss of a limb disfigures the body.
2. To mar; to impair; to injure beauty, symmetry or excellence.

To cut off from hereditary right; to deprive of an inheritance; to prevent as an heir from coming into possession of any property or right, which, by law or custom, would devolve on him the course of descent. A father sometimes disinherits his children by will. In England, the crown descendible to the eldest son, who cannot be disinherited by the will of his father.
Depriving of courage.
Not obeyed; neglected; transgressed.

displeze. [dis and please.]
1. To offend; to make angry, sometimes in slight degree. It usually expresses less than anger, vex, irritate and provoke. Applied to the Almighty in scripture, it may be considered equivalent to anger. God was displeased with this thing; therefore he smote Israel. 1 Chronicles 21:7.
2. To disgust; to excite aversion in; as, acrid and rancid substances displease the taste.
3. To offend; to be disagreeable to. distorted figure displeases the eye.

To put out of possession, by any means; to deprive of the actual occupancy of a thing, particularly of land or real estate; to deseize. Ye shall dispossess the inhabitants of the land, and dwell therein. Numbers 33:53. Usually followed by of, before the thing taken away; as, to dispossess a king of his crown.

1. The act of disputing; reasoning or argumentation in opposition to something, or opposite sides; controversy in words; verbal contest, respecting the truth of some fact, opinion, proposition or argument.
2. An exercise in colleges, in which parties reason in opposition to each other, some question proposed.
One who disputes, or who given to disputes; a controvertist. Where the disputer of this world. 1 Corinthians 1:20.
Made uneasy or restless; disturbed; harassed.
Uneasiness; restlessness; disturbance of peace in body or mind.
Concealed under a false appearance; disguised.

The act of dissembling; hiding under false appearance; feigning; false pretension; hypocrisy. dissimulation may be simply concealment of the opinions, sentiments or purpose; but it includes also the assuming of false or counterfeit appearance which conceals the real opinions or purpose. dissimulation among statesmen sometimes regarded as necessary vice, or as no vice all. Let love be without dissimulation Romans 12:9.

1. The of spinning-wheel, to which bunch of flax or tow tied, and from which the thread drawn. She layeth her hands to the spindle, and her hands hold the distaff Proverbs 31:19.
2. Figuratively, woman, or the female sex. His crown usurped, distaff on the throne.
Divorce; dissolution of the marriage tie. Let him write her a bill of divorcement Deuteronomy 24:1.

The Didus, a genus of fowls of the gallinaceous order. The hooded dodo is large than a swan, with a strong hooked bill. The general color of the plumage is cinereous; the belly and thighs whitish. The head is large, and seems as if covered with a hood. The solitary dodo is a large fowl, and is said to weigh sometimes forty five pounds. The plumage is gray and brown mixed.

1. One who does; one who performs or executes; an actor; an agent.
2. One who performs what is required; one who observes, keeps or obeys, in practice. The doers of the law shall be justified. Romans 2:13.
The name of the black-beetle, the hedgechafer, a species of Scarabaeus. We usually say, the dor-beetle.
the second person of do, used int he solemn style; thou dost

1. To be delirious; to have the intellect impaired by age, so that the mind wanders or wavers; to be silly. Time has made you dote and vainly tell of arms imagined in your lonely cell.
2. To be excessively in love; usually with on or upon; to dote on, is to love to excess or extravagance. What dust we dote on, when tis man we love. Aholah dotes on her lovers, the Assyrians. Ezekiel 23:5.
3. To decay.
Regarding with excessive fondness.
the old of drive. We now use drove.

species of camel, called also the Arabian camel, with one bunch or protuberance on the back, in distinction from the Bactrian camel, which has two bunches. It has four callous protuberances on the fore legs, and two on the hind ones. It is common beast of burden in Egypt, Syria, and the neighboring countries.

Dropsy.pronunciation: DROPSY, [L, Gr., water; the face. Formerly written hydropisy; whence by contraction, dropsy ]

definitions.text: In medicine, an unnatural collection of water, in an part of the body, proceeding from a greater effusion of serum by the exhalant arteries, than the absorbents take up. It occurs most frequently in persons of lax habits, or in bodies debilitated by disease. The dropsy takes different names, according to the part affected; as ascites, or dropsy of the abdomen; hydrocephalus, or water in the head; anasarca, or a watery swelling over the whole body; etc.
Without wetting the feet. Isaiah 11:15.
An instrument of music played by striking brass wires with little sticks. Daniel 3:5.

Dunghill
1. A heap of dung.
2. A mean or vile abode.
3. Any mean situation or condition. He lifteth the beggar from the dunghill 1 Samuel 2:8.
4. A term of reproach for a man meanly born. [Not used.]
Sprung from the dunghill; mean; low; base; vile.
Of dare.
Having ears; having spikes formed, as corn.

Earing
In seamen's language, small rope employed to fasten the upper corner of sail to its yard.
E'ARING,
plowing of land. Genesis 45:6.

1. building up, in moral and religious sense; instruction; improvement and progress of the mind, in knowledge, in morals, or in faith and holiness. He that prophesieth, speaketh to men to edification 1 Corinthians 14:3.
2. Instruction; improvement of the mind in any species of useful knowledge.
Instructed; improved in literary, moral or religious knowledge.

Edify.pronunciation: ED'IFY, [Latin oedifico; oedes, a house, and facio, to make.]

definitions.text:
1. To build, in a literal sense. [Not now used.
2. To instruct and improve the mind in knowledge generally, and particularly in moral and religious knowledge, in faith and holiness. one another. 1 Thessalonians 5:11.
3. To teach or persuade. [Not used.]]
Building up christian knowledge; instructing; improving the mind.
With effect; efficaciously; in manner to produce the intended effect; thoroughly. The weeds on land for grain must be effectually subdued. The city is effectually guarded.

Effeminate.pronunciation: EFFEM'INATE, [Latin effoeminatus, from effoeminor, to grow or make womanish, from foemina, a woman. See Woman.]

definitions.text:
Effeminate
[Latin effoeminatus, from effoeminor, to grow or make womanish, from foemina, woman. See Woman.]
1. Having the qualities of the female sex; soft or delicate to an unmanly degree; tender; womanish; voluptuous. The king, by his voluptuous life and mean marriage, became effeminate and less sensible of honor.
2. Womanish; weak; resembling the practice or qualities of the sex; as an effeminate peace; an effeminate life.
3. Womanlike, tender, sense not reproachful.
To make womanish; to unman; to weaken; as to effeminate children.
To grow womanish or weak; to melt into weakness. In slothful peace courage will effeminate
'atieth. [from eighty.] The next in order to the seventy ninth; the eighth tenth.

emb'am.
1. To open dead body, take out the intestines, and fill their place with odoriferous and desiccative spices and drugs, to prevent its putrefaction.
Joseph commanded his servants, the physicians, to embalm his father; and the physicians embalmed Israel. Genesis 50:2.
2. To fill with sweet scent.
3. To preserve, with care and affection, from loss or decay.
The memory of my beloved daughter is embalmed in my heart.
Virtue alone, with lasting grace, Embalms the beauties of the face.
Filled with aromatic plants for preservation; preserved from loss or destruction.
To border with ornamental needle-work, or figures; to adorn with raised figures of needle-work; as cloth, stuffs or muslin. Thou shalt embroider the coat of fine line. Exodus 28:39.
One who embroiders.

With a plural termination. [Corrupted from hemorrhoids, Gr. to labor under a flowing of blood.] Hemorrhoids; piles; a dilatation of the veins about the rectum, with a discharge of blood. The Lord will smite thee--with the emerods Deuteronomy 28:27.

Encamp
[from camp.] To pitch tents or form huts, as an army; to halt on march, spread tents and remain for night or for longer time, as an army or company.
They encamped in Etham. Exodus 13:20.
The Levites shall encamp about the tabernacle. Numbers 1:50.
1. To pitch tents for the purpose of siege; to besiege.
ENCAMP against the city and take it. 2 Samuel 12:28.
ENCAMP',
To form into camp; to place marching army or company in temporary habitation or quarters.
Settled in tents or huts for lodging or temporary habitation.
Pitching tents or forming huts, for temporary lodging or rest.

One who enchants; sorcerer or magician; one who has spirits or demons at his command; one who practices enchantment, or pretends to perform surprising things by the agency of demons.
1. One who charms or delights. Enchanter's nightshade, genus of plants, the Circaea.

To bring loss or to; to harm; to injure; to mischief; to prejudice.
The trial hath endamaged thee no way.
So thou shalt endamage the revenue of the kings. Ezra 4:13.
Engrave
engraved;

engraved or engraven.
Literally, to scratch or scrape. Hence,
1. To cut, as metals, stones or other hard substances, with chisel or graver; to cut figures, letters or devices, on stone or metal; to mark by incision.
Thou shalt engrave the two stones with the names of the children of Israel. Exodus  28:11.
2. To picture or represent by incisions.
3. To imprint; to impress deeply; to infix.
Let the laws of God and the principles of morality be engraved on the minds in early years.
4. To bury; to deposit in the grave; to inter; to inhume. [Not now used.]
Cut or marked, as with chisel or graver; imprinted; deeply impressed.

Enjoin.pronunciation: ENJOIN', [Latin injungo. See Join. We observe that the primary sense of join is to set, extend or lay to, to throw to or on; otherwise the sense of order or command could not spring from it. To enjoin is to set or lay to or on.]

definitions.text:
1. To order or direct with urgency; to admonish or instruct with authority; to command. Says Johnson, 'this word is more authoritative than direct, and less imperious than command.' It has the force of pressing admonition with authority; as, a parent enjoins on his children the duty of obedience. But it has also the sense of command; as the duties enjoined by God the moral law.
2. In law, to forbid judicially; to issue or direct a legal injunction to stop proceedings. This is a suit to enjoin the defendants from disturbing the plaintiffs.
Ordered; directed; admonished with authority; commanded.

Ensample
[ Latin exemplum.] An example; pattern or model for imitation. Being ensamples to the flock. 1 Peter 5:3.
To exemplify; to shew by example. This word is seldom used, either as
or verb. [See Example.]

To twist or interweave in such manner as not to be easily separated; to make confused or disordered; as, thread, yarn or ropes may be entangled; to entangle the hair.
1. To involve in any thing complicated, and from which it is difficult to extricate one's self; as, to entangle the feet in net, or in briers.
2. To lose in numerous or complicated involutions, as in labyrinth.
3. To involve in difficulties; to perplex; to embarrass; as, to entangle nation in alliances.
4. To puzzle; to bewilder; as, to entangle the understanding.
5. To insnare by captious questions; to catch; to perplex; to involve in contradictions.
The Pharisees took counsel how they might entangle him in his talk. Matthew 22:15.
6. To perplex or distract, as with cares.
No man that warreth entangleth himself with the affairs of this life. 2 Timothy  2:4.
7. To multiply intricacies and difficulties.

To make an earnest petition or request. The Janizaries entreated for them, as valiant men.
1. To offer treaty. [Not used.]
2. To treat; to discourse. [Not used.]

Earnestly supplicated, besought or solicited; importuned; urgently requested.
1. Prevailed on by urgent solicitation; consenting to grant what is desired.
2. Used; managed.

1. To surround; to encompass; to encircle; as a plain environed with mountains.
2. To involve; to envelop; as, to environ with darkness, or with difficulties.
3. To besiege; as a city environed with troops.
4. To inclose; to invest. That soldier, that man of iron, Whom ribs of horror all environ

Envying
Feeling uneasiness at the superior condition and happiness of another.

Mortification experienced at the supposed prosperity and happiness of another.
1. Ill will at others, on account of some supposed superiority. Galatians 5:21.

In Jewish antiquity, a part of the sacerdotal habit, being a kind of girdle, which was brought from behind the neck over the two shoulders, and hanging down before, was put across the stomach, then carried round the waist and used as a girdle to the tunic. There were two sorts; one of plain linen, the other embroidered for the high priest. On the part in front were two precious stones, on which were engraved the names of the twelve tribes of Israel. Before the breast was a square piece or breastplate.
plural The act of contracting or affiancing man and woman to each other; contract or mutual promise of marriage. I remember thee, the kindness of thy youth, the love of thine espousals

Espy
[Latin specio.]
1. To see at a distance; to have the first sight of a thing remove. Seamen espy land as they approach it.
2. To see or discover something intended to be hid, or in a degree concealed and not very visible; as, to espy a man in a crowd, or a thief in a wood.
3. To discover unexpectedly.
As one of them opened his sack, he espied his money. Genesis 42:27.
4. To inspect narrowly; to examine and make discoveries.
Moses sent me to espy out the land, and I brought him word again. Joshua 14:7.
ESPY',

To look narrowly; to look about; to watch.
Stand by the way and espy Jeremiah 48:19.
[This word is often pronounced spy, which see.]
ESPY',
A spy; a scout.
Valuing; estimating; valuing highly; prizing; thinking; deeming.
A tempestuous wind, which drove ashore, Malta, the ship in which Paul was sailing to Italy. It is supposed to have blown from an easterly point. Acts 27:14.

Always; eternally. Religion prefers the pleasures which flow from the presence of God for evermore
1. Always; at all times; as evermore guided by truth.

One who does evil; one who commits sin, crime, or any moral wrong. They speak evil against you as evildoers. Pet.
2.

The of demanding with authority, and compelling to pay or yield; authoritative demand; levying or drawing from by force; driving to compliance; as the exaction of tribute or of obedience.
1. Extortion; wresting from one unjustly; the taking advantage of one's necessities, to compel him to pay illegal or exorbitant tribute, fees or rewards. Take away your exactions from my people. Ezekiel 45:9.
2. That which is exacted; tribute, fees, rewards or contributions demanded or levied with severity or injustice. Kings may be enriched by exactions, but their power is weakened by the consequent disaffection of their subjects.

egzolt'. [Low Latin exalto; ex and altus, high.]
1. To raise high; to elevate.
2. To elevate in power, wealth, rank or dignity; as, to exalt one to throne, to the chief magistracy, to bishopric.
3. To elevate with joy or confidence; as, to be exalted with success or victory. [We now use elate.]
4. To raise with pride; to make undue pretensions to power, rank or estimation; to elevate too high or above others. He that exalteth himself shall be abased. Luke 14:11. Matthew 23:12.
5. To elevate in estimation and praise; to magnify; to praise; to extol. He is my father's God, and I will exalt him. Exodus 15:2.
6. To raise, as the voice; to raise in opposition. 2 Kings 19:22.
7. To elevate in diction or sentiment; to make sublime; as exalted strains.
8. In physics, to elevate; to purify; to subtilize; to refine; as, to exalt the juices or the qualities of bodies.
The act of cursing; curse pronounced; imprecation of evil; utter detestation expressed. Cease, gentle queen, these execrations:

definitions.type: verb transitive egzhort'. [Latin exhortor; ex and hortor, to encourage, to embolden, to cheer, to advise. The primary sense seems to be to excite to give strength, spirit courage.]
definitions.text:
1. To incite by words advice; to animate urge by arguments to a good deed to any laudable conduct course of action. I exhort you to be of good cheer. Acts 27:22. Young men also exhort to be sober minded. servants to be obedient to their masters. Titus 2:6.
2. To advise; to warn; to caution.
3. To incite stimulate to exertion.
To deliver exhortation; to use words arguments to incite to good deeds. And with many other words did he testify and exhort Acts 2:40.

Extol.pronunciation: EXTOL', [Latin extollo; ex and tollo, to raise.]

definitions.text: To raise in words or eulogy; praise; exalt in commendation; magnify. We extol virtues, noble exploits, and heroism. Men are too much disposed extol the rich and despise the poor. him that rideth upon the heavens by his name Jah. Psalms 68:4.
Exalted in commendation; praised; magnified.
One who practices extortion. Extortioners shall not inherit the kingdom of God. 1 Corinthians 6:10.

Fain
1. Glad; pleased; rejoiced. but the appropriate sense of the word is, glad or pleased to do something under some kind of necessity; that is, glad to evade evil or secure good. Thus, says Locke, 'The learned Castalio was fain to make trenches at Basil, to keep himself from starving.' this appropriation of the word, which is modern, led Dr. Johnson into mistake defining the word. The proper signification is glad, joyful.
FAIN,

Gladly; with joy or pleasure.
He would fain flee out of his hand. Job 27:22.
He would fain have filled his belly with husks. Luke 15:16.
FAIN,
to wish or desire. [Not used.]
Cowardly; timorous; dejected; easily depressed, or yielding to fear. Fear not, neither be fainthearted Isaiah 7:4.

1. The state of being faint; loss of strength, color and respiration.
2. Feebleness; languor; want of strength.
3. Inactivity; want of vigor.
4. Feebleness, as of color or light.
5. Feebleness of representation; as faintness of description.
6. Feebleness of mind; timorousness; dejection; irresolution. will send faintness into their hearts. Leviticus 26:36.
Counterfeiting; forging; lying; proving to be false; violating.

Famish
[Latin fames.]
1. To starve; to kill or destroy with hunger.
2. To exhaust the strength of, by hunger or thirst; to distress with hunger. The pains of famished Tantalus he'll feel.
3. To kill by deprivation or denial of any thing necessary for life.

1. To die of hunger. More generally,
2. To suffer extreme hunger or thirst; to be exhausted in strength, or to come near to perish, for want of food or drink. You are all resolved rather to die, than to famish
3. To be distressed with want; to come near to perish by destitution. The Lord will not suffer the righteous to famish Proverbs 10:3.
Starved; exhausted by want of sustenance.
lamb, kid or other young animal fattened for slaughter; fat animal; applied to quadrupeds whose flesh is used for food. David sacrificed oxen and fatlings. 2 Samuel 6:13.

1. The quality of being fat, plump, or full fed; corpulency; fullness of flesh. Their eyes stand out with fatness Psalms 73:7.
2. Unctuous or greasy matter.
3. Unctuousness; sliminess; applied to earth:hence richness; fertility; fruitfulness. God give thee of the dew of heaven, and the fatness of the earth, and plenty of corn and wine. Genesis 27:28.
4. That which gives fertility. Thy paths drop fatness Psalms 65:11. The clouds drop fatness
5. The privileges and pleasures of religion; abundant blessings. Let your soul delight itself in fatness Isaiah 55:2.

1. Timorousness; timidity.
2. State of being afraid; awe; dread. thing that makes government despised, is fearfulness of, and mean compliances with, bold popular offenders.
3. Terror; alarm; apprehension of evil. hath surprised the hypocrites. Isaiah 33:14.
Entertained sumptuously; delighted.

1. Weakness of body or mind, from any cause; imbecility; infirmity; want of strength, physical or intellectual; as feebleness of the body or limbs; feebleness of the mind or understanding.
2. Want of fullness or loudness; as feebleness of voice.
3. Want of vigor or force; as feebleness of exertion, or of operation.
4. Defect of brightness; as feebleness of light or color.

fane. [Latin fingo. The Latin forms fictum, fictus, whence figura, figure, also fucus.]
1. To invent or imagine; to form an idea or conception of something not real. There are no such things done as thou sayest, but thou feignest them out of thine own heart. Nehemiah 6:8.
2. To make a show of; to pretend; to assume a false appearance; to counterfeit. pray thee, feign thyself to be a mourner. 2 Samuel 14:2. She feigns a laugh.
3. To represent falsely; to pretend; to form and relate a fictitious tale. The poet did feign that Orpheus drew trees, stones, and floods.
4. To dissemble; to conceal. obsolete
In fiction; in pretense; not really.

1. Smallness of number; paucity.
2. Paucity of words; brevity. [Not used.]

1. The state of being filthy.
2. Foulness; dirtiness; filth; nastiness.
Carry forth the filthiness out of the holy place. 2 Chronicles 29:5.
3. Corruption; pollution; defilement by sin; impurity.
Let us cleanse ourselves from all filthiness of the flesh and spirit, perfecting holiness the fear of God. 2 Corinthians 7:1.

1. Clarifying; refining; purifying; defecating; separating from extraneous matter.
2. [See Fine, the noun. Imposing a fine or pecuniary penalty.]

1. piece of wood kindled or on fire.
2. An incendiary; one who inflames factions, or causes contention mischief.

Firstling
produced; as firstling males. Deuteronomy 15:19.

1. The first produce or offspring; applied to beasts; as the firstlings of cattle.
2. The thing first thought or done. [Not used.] The very firstlings of my heart shall be the firstlings of my hand.

1. Suitably; properly; with propriety. A maxim fitly applied.
2. Commodiously; conveniently.
vessel with narrow mouth, used for holding and conveying liquors. Stay me with flagons, comfort me with apples; for I am sick of love.

Flay.pronunciation: FLAY, [Gr. whence bark, rind; probably a contracted word.]

definitions.text:
1. To skin; to strip off the skin of an animal; as, to flay an ox.
2. To take off the skin or surface of any thing. [Not used.]
Skinned; stripped of the skin.

Kindle
[Latin accendo; from the root of candeo, caneo, to be light or white, to shine.]
1. To set on fire; to cause to burn with flame; to light; as, to kindle a fire.
2. To inflame, as the passions; to exasperate; to rouse; to provoke; to excite to action; to heat; to fire; to animate; as, to kindle anger or wrath; to kindle resentment; to kindle the flame of love, or love into a flame.
So is a contentious woman to kindle strife. Proverbs 26:21.
3. To bring forth.
KIN'DLE,

To take fire; to begin to burn with flame. Fuel and fire well laid, will kindle without a bellows.
1. To begin to rage, or be violently excited; to be roused or exasperated.
It shall kindle the thickets of the forest. Isaiah 9:18
 
Editors Note: There is an error this entry which comes from the original historical text. The reference to Proverbs 26:21 should read: So is a contentious man to kindle strife.
plural of cow. But cows, the regular plural, is now general use.

Kingly
Belonging to a king; suitable to a king; as a kingly couch.
1. Royal; sovereign; monarchical; as a kingly government.
2. Noble; august; splendid; becoming a king; as kingly magnificence.
With an air of royalty; with a superior dignity. Low bow'd the rest; he, kingly did but nod.
Relations; kindred; persons of the same family.
female relation.
ne'aded. Worked and pressed together.
nop. [a different spelling of knap or nob.] A knob; a tufted top; a bud; a bunch; a button.
Lade
laded;

laded, laden.
1. To load; to put on or in, as burden or freight. We lade ship with cotton. We lade horse or other beast with corn. And they laded their asses with the corn and departed thence. Genesis 42:26.
2. To dip; to throw in or out, as fluid, with ladle or dipper; as, to lade water out of tub or into cistern.
3. To draw water. [Not in use.]
The mouth of river. obsolete

Laded
1. Loaded; charged with burden or freight.
2.
Oppressed; burdened.

1. Expression of sorrow; cries of grief; the act of bewailing. In Rama was there voice heard, lamentation and weeping. Matthew 2:18.
2. In the plural, book of Scripture, containing the lamentations of Jeremiah.

Languish.pronunciation: LAN'GUISH, [Latin langueo, lachinisso; Gr. to flag, to lag. Latin laxo, laxus, flacceo.]

definitions.text:
Languish
[Latin langueo, lachinisso; Gr. to flag, to lag. Latin laxo, laxus, flacceo.]
1. To lose strength or animation; to be or become dull, feeble or spiritless; to pine; to be or to grow heavy. We languish under disease or after excessive exertion. She that hath borne seven languisheth. Jeremiah 15:9.
2. To wither; to fade; to lose the vegetating power. For the fields of Heshbon languisheth. Jeremiah 15:9.
3. To grow dull; to be no longer active and vigorous. The war languished for want of supplies. Commerce, agriculture, manufactures languish not for want of money, but for want of good markets.
4. To pine or sink under sorrow or any continued passion; as, woman languishes for the loss of her lover. Therefore shall the land mourn, and every one that dwelleth therein shall languish Hosea
4.
5. To look with softness or tenderness, as with the head reclined and peculiar cast of the eye.
To cause to droop or pine. [Little Used.]
Act of pining; also, soft and tender look or appearance. And the blue languish of soft Allia's eye.
bird of the genus Tringa; the tewit.

1. Bigness; bulk; magnitude; as the largeness of an animal.
2. Greatness; comprehension; as the largeness of mind or of capacity.
3. Extent; extensiveness; as largeness of views.
4. Extension; amplitude; liberality; as the largeness of an offer; largeness of heart.
5. Wideness; extent; as the largeness of river.

1. Looseness; irregular indulgence of animal desires; wantonness; lustfulness.
Who, being past feeling, have given themselves over to lasciviousness Ephesians  4:19.
2. Tendency to excite lust, and promote irregular indulgences.
The reason pretended by Augustus was, the lasciviousness of his Elegies and his Art of Love.
The string that fastens shoe. Mark 1:7.
vessel for washing; large basin; in scripture history, basin placed in the court of the Jewish tabernacle, where the officiating priests washed their hands and feet and the entrails of victims.
One who makes or enacts law; legislator.

1. Destitution of fat; want of flesh; thinness of body; meagerness; applied to animals.
2. Want of matter; poverty; emptiness; as the leanness of purse.
3. In Scripture, want of grace and spiritual comfort. He sent leanness into their soul. Psalms 106:15.

Leaved.pronunciation: LE'AVED, [from leaf; but leafed would be preferable.]

definitions.text:
1. Furnished with foliage or leaves.
2. Having leaf, or made with leaves or folds; as two-leaved gate.

Leaven
lev'n. [Latin levo, Eng. to lift.]
1. mass of sour dough, which, mixed with larger quantity of dough or paste, produces fermentation in it and renders it light. During the seven days of the passover, no leaven was permitted to be in the houses of the Jews. Exodus 12:15.
2. Any thing which makes general change in the mass. It generally means something which corrupts or depraves that with which it is mixed.
Beware of the leaven of the Pharisees and of the Sadducees. Matthew 16:6.
LEAVEN,

lev'n.
1. To excite fermentation in; to raise and make light, as dough or paste.
A little leaven leaveneth the whole lump. 1 Corinthians 5:6.
2. to taint; to imbue.
lev'ened. Taised and made light by fermentation.
Infected with leprosy; covered with white scales. His hand was leprous as snow. Exodus 4:6.

1. An aquatic animal, described in Job 41:1, and mentioned in other passages of Scripture. In Isaiah, it is called the crooked serpent. It is not agreed what animal is intended by the writers, whether the crocodile, the whale, or species of serpent.
2. The whale, or great whale.

Levite.pronunciation: LE'VITE, [from Levi, one the sons of Jacob.]

definitions.text: One of the tribe or family of Levi; a descendant of Levi; more particularly, an officer in the Jewish church, who was employed in manual service, as in bringing wood and other necessaries for the sacrifices. The Levites also sung and played on instruments of music. They were subordinate to the priests, the descendants of Aaron, who was also of the family of Levi.

1. Belong to the Levites, or descendants of Levi; as the levitical law, the law given by Moses, which prescribed the duties and rights of the priests and Levites, and regulated the civil and religious concerns of the Jews.
2. Priestly.

1. With the unlawful indulgence of lust; lustfully.
2. Wickedly; wantonly.

1. The unlawful indulgence of lust; fornication, or adultery.
2. In Scripture, it generally denotes idolatry.
3. Licentiousness; shamelessness.

1. Munificence; bounty. That liberality is but cast away, which makes us borrow what we cannot pay.
2. particular act of generosity; donation; gratuity. In this sense, has the plural number. prudent man is not impoverished by his liberalities.
3. Largeness of mind; catholicism; that comprehensiveness of mind which includes other interests beside its own, and duly estimates in its decisions the value or importance of each. It is evidence of noble mind to judge of men and things with liberality Many treat the gospel with indifference under the name of liberality
4. Candor; impartiality.
A kind of precious stone.

li'kn.
To compare; to represent as resembling or similar.
Whosoever heareth these sayings of mine, and doeth them, will liken him unto a wise man, that built his house on a rock. Matthew 7:24.
Like a lion; fierce.

Locust
[Latin locusta.] An insect of the genus Gryllus. These insects are at times so numerous in Africa and the S. of Asia as to devour every green thing, and when they migrate, they fly in an immense cloud.
A name of several plants and trees; as a species of Melianthus, and of Ceratonia.

1. Height; elevation place or position; altitude; as the loftiness a mountain.
2. Pride; haughtiness. Augustus and Tiberius had loftiness enough their tempers -
3. Elevation attitude or mien; as loftiness carriage.
4. Sublimity; elevation diction or sentiment. Three poets three distant ages born; the first loftiness thought surpass'd; the next majesty; both the last.
Bearing injuries or provocation for a long time; patient; not easily provoked. The Lord God, merciful and gracious, long-suffering and abundant goodness. Exodus 34:6.
Setting free from confinement.

Lop.pronunciation: LOP, [Eng. flap. The primary sense is evidently to fall or fell, or to strike down, and I think it connected with flap.]

definitions.text:
Lop
[Eng. flap. The primary sense is evidently to fall or fell, or to strike down, and I think it connected with flap.]
1. To cut off, as the top or extreme part of any thing; to shorten by cutting off the extremities; as, to lop a tree or its branches. With branches lopp'd in wood, or mountain fell'd.
2. To cut off, as exuberances; to separate, as superfluous parts. Expunge the whole, or lop the excrescent parts.
3. to cut partly off and bend down; as, to lop the trees or saplings of a hedge.
4. To let fall; to flap; as, a horse lops his ears.
that which is cut from trees. Else both body and lop will be of little value.
a flea. [Local.]

To hate; to look on with hatred or abhorrence; particularly, to feel disgust at food or drink, either from natural antipathy, or a sickly appetite, or from satiety, or from its ill taste. [See lothe ]
Hatred; abhorred; turned from with disgust.

Lothing
1. Feeling disgust at; having extreme aversion to; as lothing food.
2. Hating; abhorring; as lothing sin.
LO'THING,
Extreme disgust; abhorrence. Ezekiel 16:5.

Lowing
Bellowing, as an ox.
the bellowing or cry of cattle.

1. Freedom from pride; humility; humbleness of mind. Walk - with all lowliness and meekness. Ephesians 4:2. Philippians 2:3.
2. Meanness; want of dignity; abject state. [In this sense Little Used.]

lu'ker. [Latin lucrum.] Gain in money or goods; profit; usually in an ill sense, or with the sense of something base or unworthy. The lust of lucre and the dread of death. A bishop must be blameless - not given to filthy lucre Titus 1:7.

Lusting
Having eager desire; having carnal appetite.
Eager desire; inordinate desire; desire of carnal gratification.
Crippled; disabled in limbs; lame.
One who commits crime; one guilty of violating the laws, in such manner as subject him public prosecution and punishment, particularly capital punishment; criminal.
The quality of being malicious; extreme enmity or disposition to injure; malignity.

Extreme enmity, or evil dispositions of heart towards another; malice without provocation, or malevolence with baseness of heart; deep rooted spite.
1. Virulence; destructive tendency; as the malignity of an ulcer or disease.
2. Extreme evilness of nature; as the malignity of fraud.
3. Extreme sinfulness; enormity or heinousness; as the malignity of sin.

Riches; wealth; or the god of riches.
Ye cannot serve God and mammon Matthew 6:24.
One that has slain human being. The Israelites had cities of refuge for manslayers.

The Lord comes or has come; word used by the apostle Paul in expressing curse. This word was used in anathematizing persons for great crimes; as much as to say, 'may the Lord come quickly to take vengeance on thee for thy crimes.'
tool grub up weeds or roots; grubbing hoe.

1. The stomach of brutes; applied to the stomach of human beings in contempt only.
2. The craw of fowls.

Softness of temper; mildness; gentleness; forbearance under injuries and provocations.
1. In an evangelical sense, humility; resignation; submission to the divine will, without murmuring or peevishness; opposed to pride, arrogance and refractoriness. Galatians 5:23.
I beseech you by the meekness of Christ. 2 Corinthians 10:1.
Meekness is a grace which Jesus alone inculcated, and which no ancient philosopher seems to have understood or recommended.

Menstruous.pronunciation: MEN'STRUOUS, [Latin menstruus, from mensis, a month.]

definitions.text:
1. Having the monthly flow or discharge; as a female.
2. Pertaining to the monthly flow of females.

Mete
[Latin metior; Heb. to measure.] To measure; to ascertain quantity, dimensions or capacity by any rule or standard. [Obsolescent.]
Measure; limit; boundary; used chiefly in the plural, in the phrase, metes and bounds.
yard, staff or rod, used as measure. [We now use yard.]

Being in the middle, or nearest the middle of a number of things that are near the middle. If a thing is in the middle, it cannot be more so, and in this sense the word is improper. But when two or more things are near the middle, one may be nearer than another.
Giving milk; as a milch cow. It is now applied only to beasts.
Cutting into small pieces; speaking or walking affectedly.
To lessen; to diminish. [See Diminish.]
Served; afforded; supplied.

Attending and serving as a subordinate agent; serving under superior authority. Hebrews 1:14.
1. Affording aid or supplies; administering things needful.

The act of performing service as subordinate agent; agency; intervention for aid or service.
-- Because their widows were neglected the daily ministrations. Acts 6:1.
1. Office of minister; service; ecclesiastical function.
As soon as the days of his ministration were ended. Luke 1:23.

Abounding with deep mud; full of mire; as a miry road; a miry lane.
1. Consisting of mire.

Failing of the intended effect; suffering abortion. Hosea
9.
One that mocks; a scorner; a scoffer; a derider.
Softened; appeased.
A lamp light.

Mortify.pronunciation: MOR'TIFY, [Latin mors, death, and facio, to make.]

definitions.text:
Mortify
[Latin mors, death, and facio, to make.]
1. To destroy the organic texture and vital functions of some part of a living animal; to change to sphacelus gangrene. Extreme inflammation speedily mortifies flesh.
2. To subdue bring into subjection, as the bodily appetites by abstinence rigorous severities. We mortify ourselves with fish. With fasting mortified, worn out with tears.
3. To subdue; to abase; to humble; to reduce; to restrain; as inordinate passions. thy learned lust. therefore your members which are upon the earth. Colossians 3:5.
4. To humble; to depress; to affect with slight vexation. How often is the ambitious man mortified with the very praises he receives, they do not rise so high as he thinks they ought. He is controlled by a nod, mortified by a frown, and transported with a smile.
5. To destroy active powers essential qualities. He mortified pearls in vinegar--Quicksilver--mortified with turpentine.[I believe this application is not now in use.]

To lose vital heat and action and suffer the dissolution of organic texture, as flesh; to corrupt gangrene.
1. To be subdued.
2. To practice severities and penance from religious motives. This makes him give alms of all that he hath, watch, fast and mortify

in folkmote, etc. signifies a meeting. A small particle; any thing proverbially small; a spot. Why beholdest thou the mote in thy brother's eye? Matthew 7:3. The little motes in the sun do ever stir, though there is no wind. for mought, might or must, obsolete.
Eaten by moths. Job 13:1.

One that mourns or is grieved at any loss or misfortune.
1. One that follows a funeral in the habit of mourning.
2. Something used at funerals. The mourner eugh and builder oak were there.

Munition.pronunciation: MUNI'TION, [Latin munitio, from munio, to fortify.]

definitions.text:
1. Fortification.
2. Ammunition; whatever materials are used in war for defense, or for annoying an enemy. The word includes guns of all kinds, mortars, etc. and their loading.
3. Provisions of a garrison or fortress, or for ships of war, and in general for an army; stores of all kinds for a fort, an army or navy. Munition-ships, ships which convey military and naval stores of any kind, and attend or follow a fleet to supply ships of war.
mur'rin. [Latin morior, to die.] An infectious and fatal disease among cattle. Exodus 9:3.

A plant of the genus Myrtus, of several species. The common myrtle rises with a shrubby upright stem, eight or ten feet high. Its branches form a close full head, closely garnished with oval lanceolate leaves. It has numerous small, pale flowers from the axillas, singly on each footstalk.
ppr. Fastening with nails; studding.
In botany, the daffodil, genus of plants of several species. They are of the bulbous rooted tribe, perennial in root, but with annual leaves and flower stalks.

Naughtiness
Nautiness.
1. Badness; wickedness; evil principle or purpose. know thy pride and the naughtiness of thy heart. 1 Samuel 17:28.
2. Slight wickedness of children; perverseness; mischievousness.,

Nauty.
1. Wicked; corrupt. naughty person, wicked man, walketh with froward mouth. Proverbs 6:1.
2. Bad; worthless. The other basket had very naughty figs. Jeremiah 24:1.
3. Mischievous; perverse; froward; as naughty child. It is now seldom used except the latter sense, as applied to children.
jew who professed extraordinary purity of life and devotion.
One who pretends to foretell future events by holding converse with departed spirits; conjurer.
Necessary, as supply or relief; requisite. All things needful for defense abound.
Lowest; as nethermost hell; nethermost abyss.

month of the Jewish calendar, the first month of the sacred year and seventh of the civil year, answering nearly to our March. It was originally called Abibi, but began to be called nisan after the captivity.
Spread by report; much talked of.

1. Noxious to health; hurtful; mischievous; unwholesome; insalubrious; destructive; as noisome winds; noisome effluvia or miasmata; noisome pestilence.
2. Noxious; injurious.
3. Offensive to the smell or other senses; disgusting; fetid. Foul breath noisome

Noonday
Mid-day; twelve o'clock in the day.
Pertaining to mid-day; meridional; as the noonday heat.

Noontide
[See Tide, which signifies time.] The time of noon; mid-day.
Pertaining to noon; meridional.
The person or thing that nourishes.
bow or courtesy; act of reverence made by inclination of the body or the knee. Genesis 37:7.

Oblation.pronunciation: OBLA'TION, [Latin oblatio, from offero; ob and fero, to bear or bring.]

definitions.text: Any thing offered or presented in worship or sacred service; an offering; sacrifice. Bring no more vain oblations. Isaiah 1:13.
Incident; any thing that happens. obsolete
That which is scoured off; hence, refuse; rejected matter; that which is vile or despised. Lamentations 3:45. 1 Corinthians 4:13.
of'ntimes. [often and times.] Frequently; often; many times.
Frequently; often.

1. age; an advanced state of life or existence; as the oldness of a man, of an elephant or a tree.
2. The state of being old, or of a long continuance; as the oldness of a building or a garment.
3. Antiquity; as the oldness of monuments.
Supposed to be the odoriferous shell of the onyxfish, or the onyx. Exodus 30:34.
A semi-pellucid gem with variously colored zones or veins, a variety of chalcedony.

Oppress.pronunciation: OPPRESS', [Latin appressus, from opprimo; ob and premo, to press.]

definitions.text:
1. To load or burden with unreasonable impositions; to treat with unjust severity, rigor or hardship; as, to oppress a nation with taxes or contributions; to oppress one by compelling him to perform unreasonable service.
2. To overpower; to overburden; as, to be oppressed with grief.
3. to sit or lie heavy on; as, excess of good oppresses the stomach.
Overburdening.

Oration.pronunciation: ORA'TION, [Latin oratio, from oro, to pray, to utter.]

definitions.text:
1. speech or discourse composed according to the rules of oratory, and spoken in public. Orations may be reduced to three kinds; demonstrative, deliberative, and judicial.
2. In modern usage, an oration differs from sermon, from an argument the bar, and from speech before deliberative assembly. The word is now applied chiefly to discourses pronounced special occasions, as funeral oration an oration some anniversary, etc. and to academic declamations.
3. harangue; public speech or address.

Ospray.pronunciation: OS'PRAY, [Latin ossifraga; as, a bone, and frango, to break; the bone-breaker.]

definitions.text: The sea-eagle, fowl of the genus Falco or hawk, of the size of peacock. This is our fish hawk. It feeds on fish which it takes by suddenly darting upon them, when near the surface of the water.
The ospray or sea-eagle. In Leviticus 11:13, it denotes different fowl.
Farthest outward; remote from the middle.
of outgo.

Overcharge
1. To or load to excess; to cloy; to oppress. The heavy load of abundance with which we overcharge nature -
2. To crowd too much. Our language is overcharged with consonants.
3. To burden.
4. To fill to excess; to surcharge; as, to overcharge the memory.
5. To load with too great charge, as gun.
6. To too much; to enter in an account more than is just. OVERCHARGE,

1. An excessive load or burden.
2. in an account of more than is just.
3. beyond what is proper.
superficial covering. Exodus 38:17.

Overmuch
Too much; exceeding what is necessary or proper.
In too great a degree.
More than sufficient.

1. To cross; to go over.
2. To overlook; to without regard.
3. To omit, in reckoning.
4. To omit; not to receive or include.

Surplus; that which remains after a supply, or beyond a quantity proposed. Take what is wanted and return the overplus It would look like a fable to report that this gentleman gives away all which is the overplus of a great fortune.
